Name: USS Oriskany Reef
Design: Scuttled aircraft carrier
Description: World's largest intentionally sunk artificial reef; a decommissioned WWII-era carrier scuttled in 2006, now teeming with pelagic and reef fish at 70–145 ft depth.

Name: USNS General Hoyt S. Vandenberg
Design: Scuttled military transport ship
Description: Second-largest artificial reef in the world; a Cold War–era missile tracking ship sunk in 2009, resting 40–140 ft below the surface off Key West.

Name: Osborne Reef (Failed)
Design: Bundled waste tires
Description: Notorious 1970s failure; tires never colonized by coral, broke free in storms damaging natural reefs, and multi-million-dollar cleanup is still ongoing decades later.

Name: Neptune Memorial Reef
Size: 600,000 sq ft of ocean floor at 40 ft depth
Design: Underwater columbarium city with roads and statuary
Description: World's largest underwater memorial; cremated remains are cast into sculptures forming an artificial reef city opened in 2007, doubling as dive attraction.

Name: Eternal Reefs
Design: Reef Ball with cremated remains
Description: Combines cremation memorials with reef restoration; ashes are mixed into concrete Reef Balls deployed along the US Atlantic and Gulf coasts.

Name: Redbird Reef
Size: 714 subway cars, 86 tanks, 8 tugboats over 1.3 sq nautical miles
Design: Scuttled subway cars and military vehicles
Description: Largest single deployment of NYC Redbird subway cars; established 2001 at 80 ft depth, now a thriving mid-Atlantic fishing and diving destination.

Name: NYC Subway Car Reef Program
Location: Atlantic coast: NJ, DE, MD, VA, SC, GA, USA
Design: Stripped and decontaminated subway cars
Description: Decade-long program (2001–2010) deploying decommissioned Redbird and R32 subway cars as artificial reef substrate along the US East Coast.

Name: Bay County Super Reefs
Design: Concrete super reef modules
Description: Massive prefabricated concrete structures deployed in federal waters to enhance recreational fishing and diving in the Gulf of Mexico.

Name: Palm Beach County Reef Program
Design: Ships and limestone boulders and concrete
Description: One of the most prolific county-level programs in the US, combining scuttled vessels with massive boulder and concrete deployments.

Name: Pinellas County King's Reef
Size: Multiple deployment zones in Gulf of Mexico
Design: Concrete and limestone modules
Description: Expanded in 2023 with new material locations; part of one of Florida's most active county reef programs serving recreational anglers and divers.

Name: Florida NRDA BP Oil Spill Reef Restoration
Location: Multiple sites, Florida Gulf coast, USA
Design: Prefabricated artificial reef modules
Description: One of the largest artificial reef construction projects in Florida history, funded by BP Deepwater Horizon oil spill natural resource damage assessment.

Name: Texas Artificial Reef Program
Size: 66 reef sites, 3,440 acres, 5–100 miles from shore
Design: Ships and oil platform jackets
Description: Includes 13 intentionally sunk ships and 140+ converted oil platforms; the Texas Clipper alone generates $1M+ annually for the local economy.

Name: USTS Texas Clipper
Design: Scuttled training ship
Description: Largest ship in the Texas reef program; generates over $1M from anglers and $1.4–2M from divers annually for the South Padre Island economy.

Name: Gulf of Mexico Rigs-to-Reefs Program
Design: Decommissioned oil and gas platform jackets
Description: World's largest concentration of artificial reef structures; over 600 platforms converted since 1979, with Louisiana (350+) and Texas leading adoption.

Name: Alabama Artificial Reef Program
Location: Gulf of Mexico off Alabama, USA
Size: One of the largest reef programs in the world
Design: Concrete modules and decommissioned structures
Description: Began in 1953 with car bodies; now one of the world's largest programs spanning military hardware, concrete modules, and platform jackets.

Name: South Carolina Artificial Reefs
Design: Ships/tanks/subway cars/concrete
Description: Diverse reef portfolio including army tanks, armored personnel carriers, tugboats, barges, and subway cars deployed since the 1970s.

Name: South Carolina Memorial Reef
Design: Specially managed memorial structures
Description: Only deep-water artificial reef on the US Atlantic coast with special management enforcement, combining memorial services with marine habitat creation.

Name: Georgia Artificial Reef Program
Design: Concrete and steel structures
Description: State-managed network of artificial reefs built since 1970 to provide habitats for game fish along the Georgia coast.

Name: Hawaii R3D Living Reef
Design: Engineered living coastal protection system
Description: First integrated ecosystem-level approach to design and build a living coastal-protection system inspired by natural coral reef structures.

Name: American Samoa Coral Restoration
Design: Coral transplantation with heat-tolerant strains
Description: Multi-institute collaborative leveraging 20+ years of coral heat tolerance studies to build climate-resilient restored reefs starting 2026.

Name: MUSA - Cancun Underwater Museum
Design: pH-neutral cement life-size sculptures
Description: World-famous underwater museum with 500+ sculptures functioning as artificial reef substrate; attracts 750,000+ visitors annually while diverting pressure from natural reefs.

Name: Kan-Kanán Reef
Design: ~800 hollow pyramidal cement and microsilica structures
Description: World's longest artificial reef started in 2014; designed to support an estimated 13,000 marine species along Mexico's Caribbean coast.

Name: Cozumel 3D-Printed Coral Restoration
Design: 3D-printed coral substrate structures
Description: Pioneering use of 3D-printing technology to create biomimetic structures for coral attachment and growth in the Mexican Caribbean.

Name: Cancun Seaweed-to-Reef Project
Design: 3D-printed structures from recycled sargassum seaweed
Description: Innovative circular-economy approach converting invasive sargassum seaweed into 3D-printed artificial reef structures.

Name: Molinere Underwater Sculpture Park
Design: pH-neutral cement figurative sculptures
Description: World's first underwater sculpture park (2006); created after Hurricane Ivan devastated Molinere Bay, now a thriving coral-colonized tourist attraction.

Name: Museo Atlántico
Design: Submerged sculptural and architectural forms
Description: Europe's first underwater sculpture museum (2016); over 300 works at 12–15m depth, designed to provoke environmental awareness while creating reef habitat.

Name: Barbados Reef Ball Project
Organization: Barbados Marine Trust / Reef Ball Foundation
Design: Reef Ball concrete modules
Description: Pilot deployment of 30 Reef Balls in a sandy channel between patch reefs in 2004 to enhance marine habitat connectivity.

Name: Sint Maarten Oceanite Reef
Design: Nano-engineered Oceanite mineral substrate
Description: Post-Hurricane Irma restoration using novel Oceanite mineral substrates deployed in 2018 to test rapid coral colonization technology.

Name: Buccoo Reef MOSES Project
Design: Modular Sealife System (MOSES) concrete blocks
Description: Pilot restoration of the critically degraded 7 km² Buccoo Reef, which lost 28% coral cover between 1994–2008, using innovative modular MOSES blocks.

Name: AROSSTA Project
Location: St. Eustatius and Saba, Caribbean Netherlands
Design: Artificial reef structures for coral settlement
Description: Caribbean Dutch islands pilot project testing artificial structures for coral settlement and reef ecosystem recovery.

Name: US Virgin Islands Reef Restoration
Design: Engineered reef structures
Description: $760,000+ federally funded post-hurricane coral reef restoration following Hurricanes Irma and Maria devastation.

Name: Reef Ball Foundation Global Program
Design: Hollow concrete dome (Reef Ball)
Description: World's most widely deployed artificial reef technology; patented hollow concrete domes used from tropical oceans to freshwater lakes across every continent including Antarctica.

Name: Antigua Reef Ball Deployment
Design: Reef Ball concrete modules
Description: Large-scale island-wide deployment of 4,700 Reef Ball modules to enhance marine habitat around Antigua.

Name: Campeche Mexico Reef Balls
Organization: Local fishing communities / Reef Ball Foundation
Design: Reef Ball concrete modules
Description: Community-driven deployment of 4,000+ Reef Balls to enhance local artisanal fishery resources in the Gulf of Mexico.

Name: Ocean Revival Underwater Park
Design: Four scuttled Portuguese Navy warships
Description: World's largest single artificial reef from military vessels; four ships (corvette, patrol vessel, research ship, frigate) sunk in 2012 creating a premier dive park.

Name: Algarve Reef Complex
Design: Concrete and steel reef modules
Description: Government-funded program (1990–2008) creating seven interconnected reef systems along the Algarve coast for fisheries enhancement and habitat creation.

Name: Hengistbury Head Reef
Design: Rock and reef units along groyne structures
Description: Environmental enhancement scheme combining coastal defense with marine habitat creation along a groyne system on the English Channel coast.

Name: Isle of Man Marina Reef
Location: Douglas Harbour, Isle of Man, UK
Design: Electrolytic mineral accretion reef
Description: Novel artificial reef at a £100M yacht marina using CCell's electrolytic technology for scour protection and marine habitat creation.

Name: Dutch North Sea BOOST Oyster Reef
Design: Pyramid-shaped ReefBooster concrete structures
Description: Pilot project to restore the nearly vanished flat oyster population (once 30% of Dutch North Sea seabed) using pyramid-shaped artificial substrates.

Name: German Baltic Sea Reefs
Design: Natural rocks and concrete reef cones/tetrapods/rings
Description: Combination of natural rocks and engineered concrete structures (cones, tetrapods, rings) deployed to enhance Baltic marine biodiversity.

Name: X-Reef Mediterranean
Design: 3D-printed concrete mimicking Coralligenous ecosystem
Description: 3D-printed reef designed to replicate the complex Coralligenous biogenic formations unique to the Mediterranean Sea.

Name: Dubai Reefs
Design: 3D-printed biomaterial underwater landscapes
Description: Announced as world's largest ocean restoration project; aims to create habitat for 1 billion corals and 100 million mangroves by 2040 using 3D-printed biomaterials.

Name: Abu Dhabi Coral Gardens
Design: Eco-friendly artificial coral structures of various designs
Description: Largest artificial coral project in the Middle East; installing 40,000 eco-friendly structures spanning coastal and deep waters to support marine organism growth.

Name: Project REEFrame
Design: Modular reef frame structures
Description: Launched 2021 on UAE's east coast; phase 1 created a 1-hectare reef, now expanding to a 10-hectare second reef in the Gulf of Oman.

Name: Bahrain Reef Arabia
Design: Reef Ball and custom reef units
Description: Ten reef complexes deployed to re-establish fish breeding grounds and secure the local fishing industry threatened by habitat loss in the Persian Gulf.

Name: Tamar Reef
Design: 3D-printed reef platforms with nursery-grown corals
Description: Red Sea's first artificial reef (2007); a joint Israeli-Jordanian project that diverts diver traffic from fragile natural reefs and hosts thousands of fish.

Name: Aqaba Artificial Reef
Design: Artificial reef structures with transplanted corals
Description: Collaborative Israeli-Jordanian reef extending Tamar project across the border; symbolizes both ecological restoration and cross-border scientific cooperation.

Name: India Biorock Reef Project
Location: Mithapur coast, Gulf of Kachchh, Gujarat, India
Design: Biorock mineral accretion technology
Description: Uses low-voltage electrical current to crystallize calcium carbonate on submerged structures, creating limestone similar to natural reefs that are more stress-resistant.

Name: Thailand National Reef Program
Location: Thai coastal waters (Gulf of Thailand, Andaman Sea)
Design: Concrete blocks and purpose-built structures
Description: National artificial reef program since the 1970s, expanded significantly after 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami with both fishery and conservation objectives.

Name: Malaysia National Reef Program
Design: Tire reefs and concrete structures
Description: National program since 1975 across 76+ sites; transitioning from tire-based reefs to engineered structures including converted LRT coaches.

Name: Tioman Island LRT Coach Reef
Design: Submerged light rail transit coach structures
Description: First island in Malaysia to submerge LRT coach replicas as artificial reefs (2024); recognized by the Malaysia Book of Records.

Name: Malaysia Reef Ball Sea Turtle Program
Location: Protected sea turtle nesting islands, Malaysia
Design: Reef Ball concrete modules
Description: 5,000 Reef Balls deployed around protected nesting islands to deter illegal netting, successfully increasing sea turtle nesting numbers.

Name: Singapore Sisters' Islands Marine Park Reef
Design: PVC/fibreglass frames with concrete bases
Description: Singapore's largest artificial reef project (2018); eight structures adding 500 m² of reef habitat using calcium-rich concrete frames to encourage coral attachment.

Name: Philippines Underwater Chocolate Hills
Design: Broken coral on wire structures
Description: Broken coral fragments harvested and attached to wire structures by local divers to rebuild reef habitat off Panglao Island.

Name: Philippines Concrete Cube Reefs
Design: Hollow concrete cubes in marine reserves
Description: Widespread deployment of hollow concrete cubes as coral colonization sites, often within marine reserves, as part of artisanal fishery support since 1981.

Name: Cambodia Artificial Reef Program
Design: Various concrete and steel structures
Description: Government-initiated artificial reef program since 1991 to support coastal fisheries and marine habitat in Cambodian waters.

Name: Pemuteran Biorock Project
Organization: Global Coral Reef Alliance / Karang Lestari Foundation
Design: Biorock mineral accretion technology with low-voltage electricity
Description: World's largest and longest-running coral reef restoration project; awarded UN Equator Prize 2012; transformed dead reef into pristine coral habitat since 2000.

Name: Gili Islands Biorock Complex
Design: Biorock mineral accretion technology
Description: World's largest complex of coral regeneration projects with 182 individual Biorock reef structures; daughter project of Pemuteran.

Name: Similajau Beacon Reef Project
Design: Reef Ball concrete modules
Description: Deployed 1,500 artificial Reef Balls and successfully regenerated more than 7 coral species within national park waters.

Name: MARS Reef Design Lab (Maldives)
Design: 3D-printed ceramic blocks filled with concrete (LEGO-like assembly)
Description: Modular Artificial Reef Structure (MARS) using 3D-printed ceramic-concrete blocks that divers assemble underwater like LEGOs for rapid reef restoration.

Name: Maldives MARRS Coral Restoration
Design: Mars Assisted Reef Restoration System (hexagonal metal frames)
Description: Low-tech community-based system achieving 40% increase in coral cover and 730% increase in fish abundance within 2 years.

Name: Reefscapers Maldives
Design: Metal coral propagation frames
Description: Pioneered coral frame technology in 2005; deploys metal frames seeded with coral fragments at resort islands across the Maldives.

Name: Koh Tao (New Heaven) Reef Restoration
Design: Concrete dome and block structures with coral transplants
Description: Long-running community conservation program combining artificial reef deployment with coral gardening and marine monitoring in the Gulf of Thailand.

Name: Wonder Reef
Design: Submerged floating reef flutes
Description: World's first submerged floating reef design; nine tethered sculptural flutes already colonized by dozens of fish species since deployment.

Name: Palm Beach Reef (Gold Coast)
Design: Submerged coastal protection reef
Description: Built 2019 for $18.2M AUD primarily as erosion protection; also enhances surfing conditions through engineered sandbank formation.

Name: Narrowneck Reef
Design: Geotextile sand-filled containers
Description: Pioneering multi-purpose reef (late 1990s) reducing coastal erosion and enhancing surfing conditions; scientifically validated over 20+ years of operation.

Name: Ex-HMAS Adelaide
Location: Avoca Beach, Central Coast, NSW, Australia
Design: Scuttled Oliver Perry-class frigate
Description: Scuttled in 2011 before 18,000 spectators; now a premier wreck dive though aluminium superstructure suffered galvanic corrosion and storm damage.

Name: Ex-HMAS Brisbane
Design: Scuttled destroyer
Description: Guided missile destroyer scuttled July 2005 off the Sunshine Coast to create an artificial reef and dive site.

Name: Ex-HMAS Canberra
Design: Scuttled frigate
Description: Frigate scuttled October 2009 off Ocean Grove to create an artificial reef and dive attraction in Victorian waters.

Name: HMNZS Canterbury
Design: Scuttled naval frigate
Description: Decommissioned New Zealand naval frigate scuttled to create an artificial reef and popular dive site in the Bay of Islands.

Name: HMCS Chaudière
Design: Scuttled Canadian destroyer escort
Description: First ARSBC project; Canadian destroyer escort scuttled December 1992 establishing the 'Canadian model' for ship-to-reef conversion.

Name: HMCS Mackenzie
Design: Scuttled Canadian destroyer escort
Description: Second ARSBC ship reef; scuttled September 1995 near Sidney on Vancouver Island as a dive attraction and marine habitat.

Name: HMCS Columbia
Location: Maud Island, Campbell River, BC, Canada
Design: Scuttled Canadian destroyer escort
Description: Scuttled June 1996 near Campbell River; part of ARSBC's pioneering program that influenced artificial reef practices in Australia and New Zealand.

Name: HMCS Saskatchewan
Design: Scuttled Canadian destroyer escort
Description: Scuttled June 1997 near Nanaimo; one of eight ships and one Boeing 737 sunk by ARSBC since 1991 off British Columbia's coast.

Name: Boeing 737 Reef (BC)
Design: Scuttled Boeing 737 airliner
Description: Unique aircraft-to-reef conversion; a Boeing 737 sunk as part of ARSBC's diverse artificial reef program off BC's coast.

Name: Wasini Island / REEFolution
Design: Metal coral nursery frames and artificial reef structures
Description: Large-scale coral nursery and reef restoration planting 8,000+ corals annually since 2012; one of Africa's most successful community-based reef projects.

Name: Shimoni Reef Project
Design: Engineered reef structures
Description: Targeted restoration using engineered reef structures in the ecologically important Shimoni marine area of southern Kenya.

Name: Mkwiro Community Reefs
Design: Concrete disks with bottles / layered disks / metal cages
Description: Community-managed experimental reef testing four different low-cost artificial reef designs for coral recovery in southern Kenya.

Name: Mnemba Island Reef Restoration
Design: Artificial reef structures with coral transplants
Description: Collaborative project launched 2021 achieving 80% coral coverage restoration on Mnemba's house reef; combines tourism with conservation.

Name: Reef Doctor Madagascar
Design: Concrete and rubble structures for octopus habitat
Description: Community-driven artificial reef construction in multiple villages to create octopus habitat and alternative fishing areas in Madagascar.

Name: Vamizi Island Conservation
Design: Coral restoration and reef protection
Description: Center of operations for Oceans Without Borders in the biodiverse Quirimbas Archipelago of northern Mozambique.

Name: Nature Seychelles Reef Rescuers
Size: 50,000+ coral nubbins, 5,000+ m² restored, 12 nurseries, 34 species
Design: Spider-shaped iron rebar coral frames
Description: Decade-long project raising 50,000+ coral fragments from 34 species in 12 midwater nurseries and restoring 5,000+ m² of bleaching-devastated reef.

Name: Mauritius Coral Restoration
Design: Concrete block artificial reefs with coral nurseries
Description: Part of $9.1M Adaptation Fund project; 25,000 coral fragments transplanted to 20 km² of degraded reef supporting native and rare fish species.

Name: SECORE Coral Restoration
Design: 3D-printed tetrapod seeding units
Description: Pioneering sexual coral reproduction for restoration; collects naturally spawned gametes and settles larvae onto 3D-printed substrates for reef outplanting.

Name: CyBe 3D-Printed Reef
Design: 3D concrete-printed reef modules
Description: Industrial-scale 3D concrete printing for artificial reef modules with complex geometries that mimic natural reef habitat structure.

Name: Technion 3D-Printed Clay Reef
Location: Interuniversity Institute for Marine Sciences, Eilat, Israel
Design: 3D-printed terracotta clay reef structures
Description: Academic research project using participatory design and 3D-printed fired clay to create ecologically optimized reef structures for coral settlement.

Name: Artreefs
Design: Artistic sculptural reef modules
Description: European startup combining art and marine science to create sculptural artificial reef modules for Mediterranean deployment.

Name: EU Building Artificial Reefs Project
Location: European waters (representative: Nienhagen reef, Baltic Sea, Germany)
Design: Various experimental designs
Description: EU-funded research initiative studying artificial reef effectiveness for ecosystem improvement across European marine environments.

Name: SS United States Reef
Size: 990 ft long (world's largest artificial reef by length)
Design: Scuttled ocean liner
Description: Historic 1952 ocean liner to become the world's largest artificial reef; hull transported to Florida for sinking as marine habitat and dive attraction.

Name: HMCS Yukon (MV Yukon) Reef
Organization: US Navy / City of San Diego / California Diving Council
Location: Pacific Beach, San Diego, California, USA
Size: 366 ft long (112m) destroyer escort; 85–105 ft depth
Design: Scuttled Canadian destroyer escort
Description: Decommissioned Royal Canadian Navy destroyer escort deliberately sunk in 2000 off San Diego as Southern California's premier wreck dive; 366 feet long, resting at 85–105 ft depth and colonized by sheephead, cabezon, and lingcod.

Name: Wreck Alley Complex
Organization: City of San Diego / CDFW / dive community
Location: Mission Beach, San Diego, California, USA
Size: 6 wrecks spanning ~1 sq mile at 60–75 ft depth
Design: Multiple scuttled vessels: Coast Guard cutter, barges, kelp harvester, research tower
Description: San Diego's famous multi-wreck dive destination including the Ruby E (165-ft Coast Guard cutter, 1989), El Rey kelp harvester (1987), Yukon, and NOSC Tower; one of California's richest artificial reef complexes.

Name: Wheeler North Reef
Location: San Clemente, Orange County, California, USA
Size: 380 acres of cobble/rock; world's largest kelp mitigation reef
Design: Quarry cobble reef for giant kelp habitat mitigation
Description: World's largest artificial kelp reef, constructed by Southern California Edison as mitigation for the San Onofre Nuclear Generating Station; 380 acres of quarry cobble at 20–60 ft depth, producing substantial kelp forest and rockfish habitat.

Name: Palos Verdes Reef Restoration
Organization: The Bay Foundation / NOAA / US EPA
Location: Palos Verdes Peninsula, Los Angeles County, California, USA
Size: 52,729 tons of quarry rock over ~32 acres; 50–70 ft depth
Design: Quarry rock reef for giant kelp and white seabass restoration
Description: Major reef restoration completed 2020 using 52,729 tons of quarry rock to rebuild urchin-barren seafloor at Palos Verdes into a productive kelp forest; part of broader Montrose Settlements Restoration Program funded by a DDT contamination settlement.

Name: California State Artificial Reef Program (CDFW)
Organization: California Department of Fish and Wildlife
Size: 11 permitted reef sites; 100,000+ tons of material total
Design: Quarry rock, concrete, and mixed material reefs
Category: State Program / Multi-Site Engineered Rock Reef
Description: California's formal artificial reef program with 11 sites along the coast including Pitas Point (9,078 tons, 1984), Topanga (10,000 tons, 1987), and Atascadero (3,500 tons, 1985); program currently unfunded and halted as of 2025, but existing reefs persist as productive kelp habitat.

Name: Casino Point Dive Park (Catalina Island)
Organization: City of Avalon / Catalina Island Conservancy
Location: Avalon, Santa Catalina Island, California, USA
Size: Multiple artificial structures at 15–100 ft depth; protected MPA
Design: Sunken vessels, concrete structures, and kelp restoration
Category: Engineered Dive Park / Multiple Structure Reef
Description: California's first official underwater park, established 1965; protected MPA at Avalon Harbor featuring sunken structures, abundant garibaldi, sheephead, moray eels, and opaleye; heavily visited dive site just off the Catalina Island waterfront.

Name: California Offshore Oil Platforms (Rigs-to-Reefs Candidates)
Organization: Platform operators / California State Lands Commission
Location: Santa Barbara Channel and Southern California Bight, California, USA
Size: 27 active/idle platforms; densest oil infrastructure in the US Pacific
Design: Steel jacket oil and gas production platforms functioning as de facto artificial reefs
Description: California's 27 offshore oil platforms—including Platform Holly (1966, Goleta), Hogan, Houchin, and Ellen—support among the highest fish biomass per unit area of any habitat on the Pacific coast; California AB 2503 (2010) enables formal rigs-to-reefs conversion, but no platforms have yet been formally converted.

Name: Uribe 121 / Rosarito Underwater Park
Organization: Rosarito Municipal Government / Baja California State Government
Location: El Descanso Bay, Rosarito, Baja California, Mexico
Design: Scuttled Mexican Navy Uribe-class offshore patrol vessel
Description: Baja California's first intentional artificial reef, sunk November 2015; anchor of the planned Rosarito Underwater Park featuring a ship cemetery, Mesoamerican Atlantis sculptural zone, and Titanic tribute—located just ~30 miles south of the US–Mexico border.

Name: Fang Ming Wreck Reef
Organization: Private / Baja California Sur dive community
Location: Espíritu Santo Island, La Paz, Baja California Sur, Mexico
Design: Scuttled Chinese freighter
Description: First vessel intentionally scuttled as an artificial reef in Latin America (November 1999); sited in a protected no-fishing zone off Espíritu Santo Island, now colonized by green turtles, angelfish, parrotfish, groupers, snappers, and California sea lions.

Name: C-59 / ARM Cadete Francisco Márquez Reef
Location: La Paz, Baja California Sur, Mexico (Sea of Cortez)
Design: Scuttled WWII-era US Navy Admiral-class minesweeper; transferred to Mexican Navy 1962
Description: Originally a decorated WWII US Navy minesweeper with three battle stars; transferred to Mexico in 1962 and sunk in 2004 as an artificial reef; its engine room, bridge, and crew quarters are intact and draped with soft corals, sponges, and reef fish.

Name: Salvatierra Wreck Reef
Location: La Paz, Baja California Sur, Mexico (Sea of Cortez)
Design: Ex-US Navy WWII Landing Ship Tank (LST) sunk 1976
Description: WWII US Navy landing ship that struck Swanee Rock in 1976 and sank with cargo trucks on deck; the trucks' tires remain inflated decades later; one of La Paz's most diverse dive sites, accessible to all skill levels.

Name: Shell Nurse Reef — La Paz
Organization: JICA / Ocean Construction / Satoumi Council (SEMARNAT, CONAPESCA, UABCS, fishing cooperatives)
Location: San Juan de la Costa, La Paz Bay, Baja California Sur, Mexico
Design: Japanese satoumi-model concrete-and-shell hybrid structures; 500-year design life
Description: First Shell Nurse artificial reef deployed outside Japan (May 2023); one-million-dollar JICA-funded project that recorded 24,284 organisms of 35 species—including 14 commercial species—within its first year of deployment in the Sea of Cortez.

Name: Reef Ball Project — La Paz Bay
Location: La Paz Bay, Baja California Sur, Mexico
Size: 1,500 Reef Ball units across ~684,000 sq meters in three reef zones; 30m depth
Design: Hollow hemisphere concrete Reef Balls; 500-year design life
Description: SEMARNAT-permitted deployment of 1,500 Reef Ball structures across three discrete zones in La Paz Bay, targeting sandy seafloor transformation into productive marine habitat in the biologically rich Sea of Cortez.

Name: Edmonds Underwater Park
Size: 27 acres; 2.5 miles of underwater trails; max depth 45 ft
Design: Sunken drydock, boats, concrete rubble, navigation buoys, PVC pipes
Description: Washington state's most popular artificial reef, established 1970 as a marine preserve; 27 acres of sunken structures host rockfish, kelp greenling, anemones, and bull kelp, attracting ~25,000 diver visits annually.

Name: Saltwater State Park Artificial Reef
Location: Des Moines, King County, Washington, USA
Size: Three parallel reef structures at 25–80 ft depth
Design: Boulders and concrete pipe reef structures
Description: Washington's only designated underwater dive park within a state park; three parallel reef structures within a 137-acre marine sanctuary support wolf eels, giant Pacific octopus, lingcod, plumose anemones, and large rockfish.

Name: Alki Reef (Jacobson Fishing Reef)
Size: 2.83 hectares; 181,400 metric tons quarry rock; 14 reef structures
Design: Quarry rock piles in two rows (shallow and deep)
Description: Created in 1987 as mitigation for the Elliott Bay Marina; 14 quarry rock structures at Alki Point now dominate by giant plumose anemones and provide sanctuary for copper, quillback, and brown rockfish, lingcod, and sculpins.

Name: Smitty's Cove Artificial Reef
Organization: NOAA / US Fish & Wildlife Service / Alaska Marine Lines
Location: Passage Canal near Whittier, Prince William Sound, Alaska, USA
Size: Concrete balls and pyramids (300–400 lbs each); 30–40 ft depth
Design: Engineered concrete ball and pyramid modules
Description: Alaska's first purpose-built artificial reef, installed May 2006 as mitigation for a barge ramp project; a research pilot to test artificial reef effectiveness in high-latitude subarctic conditions.

Name: Italian National Artificial Reef Program
Organization: Italian Ministry of Agriculture & Forestry / ISPRA
Design: Primarily concrete structures (70%+ of sites); some steel and mixed materials
Description: Europe's longest-running and most prolific national artificial reef program; Italy has deployed 118 reefs over 50+ years, with structures used for both fisheries enhancement and anti-trawling barriers in near-shore protected zones.

Name: Boreas Wreck Reef (Costa Brava)
Organization: Spanish maritime authorities / Girona provincial government
Design: Scuttled WWII-era tugboat (sunk 1989)
Description: Most celebrated wreck dive on the Spanish Mediterranean coast; a 40-meter German WWII tugboat deliberately sunk in 1989 off Palamós, now encrusted with gorgonian fans, nudibranchs, and large grouper populations; Spain has 61 artificial reefs nationally.

Name: Airbus A300 Reef — Kuşadası
Organization: Turkish Ministry of Tourism / Kuşadası Municipal Authorities
Design: Decommissioned Airbus A300 commercial aircraft
Description: Claimed to be the largest commercial aircraft ever sunk as an artificial reef; the 36-year-old Airbus A300 was scuttled at 23m depth off Kuşadası in 2016 to boost Aegean diving tourism.

Name: USS Kittiwake Reef
Location: Seven Mile Beach, Grand Cayman, Cayman Islands
Design: Scuttled 1945 US Navy submarine rescue ship
Description: Decommissioned US Navy submarine rescue ship sunk January 2011 off Seven Mile Beach in Grand Cayman; now one of the Caribbean's most accessible wreck dives at 15–64 ft, heavily colonized by coral and fish and accessible to snorkelers.

Name: SS Thistlegorm Wreck
Organization: Egyptian dive authorities / international marine research teams
Location: Sha'ab Ali, Strait of Gubal, Red Sea, Egypt
Size: WWII British cargo ship; 126m long at 30m depth
Design: WWII British armed cargo vessel sunk 1941 by German bombers
Description: One of the world's most celebrated artificial reef wrecks, rediscovered by Jacques Cousteau in 1956; holds motorcycles, trucks, and military equipment; long-term studies (2007–2014) confirm it supports communities as ecologically rich as natural reefs.

Name: Saudi Aramco Artificial Reef Program
Location: Arabian Gulf and Red Sea (Manifa, Jubail, Ras Tanura, Jazan), Saudi Arabia
Size: ~1,000 tons; 31+ sites across Gulf and Red Sea
Design: Engineered reef blocks and 3D-printed coral structures
Description: Corporate artificial reef and coral restoration program deploying engineered reef blocks and 3D-printed structures across 31+ sites; monitoring shows highest fish abundance at Manifa, Abu Ali, Jubail, and Ras Tanura; integrates coral transplantation.

Name: Sri Lanka Artificial Reef Program
Organization: Sri Lanka Navy / Ruhunu Diving Center / IUCN / Holcim Lanka
Location: Multiple sites including Nilwella and Unawatuna, Southern Sri Lanka
Size: 15+ structures at Unawatuna; mixed vehicle and purpose-built units
Design: Decommissioned buses, cars, statues, concrete blocks, and aircraft
Description: Eclectic reef program using decommissioned buses, cars, and aircraft as substrate; the Bar Reef system off Sri Lanka's north coast holds the highest coral biodiversity of any Indian Ocean reef; historic shipwrecks throughout coastal waters function as thriving unofficial reefs.

Name: BULA Reef (Mamanuca Islands)
Organization: Corals for Conservation (C4C) / Plantation Island Resort / UNESCO Reefs of Hope
Size: 52 ft (16m) tall, 147 ft (45m) long metal frame complex
Design: Steel frame nursery platforms seeded with heat-resistant coral colonies
Description: World's largest word written underwater, launched June 2024; houses 1,000+ heat-adapted coral colonies rescued from thermal stress zones; part of UNESCO's 'Reefs of Hope' initiative for climate-resilient coral restoration across the Pacific.

Name: North Carolina Artificial Reef Program
Organization: North Carolina Division of Marine Fisheries
Size: 43 ocean reefs and 25 estuarine reefs; 0.5–38 miles offshore
Design: Concrete pipe, Reef Balls, scuttled ships, mixed materials
Description: One of the US East Coast's most effective artificial reef programs, operating without access to offshore oil/gas rigs; AR-305 is among the most popular fishing and diving destinations on the Atlantic coast; 68 total sites span inshore and offshore environments.

Name: Great Lakes Freshwater Artificial Reefs
Organization: US and Canadian state/provincial resource agencies
Location: Lakes Michigan, Erie, Huron, Ontario, and Superior, USA/Canada
Size: 19+ documented reefs across all five Great Lakes
Design: Limestone blocks, concrete rubble, stadium demolition debris, and natural materials
Description: The world's most extensive freshwater artificial reef network; earliest reefs date to the 19th century; modern deployments since the 1980s target walleye, smallmouth bass, and lake trout spawning and aggregation habitat across all five Great Lakes.

Name: Oman National Artificial Reef Farm
Organization: Oman Ministry of Agriculture, Fisheries and Water Resources
Location: Wilayat of Suwaiq, Al-Batinah South, Oman (Arabian Sea)
Size: 4,280 reef units; 20 km coastline × 7 km width; 15–30m depth
Design: Concrete Arab pyramid, cube, and steel models; supplemented by traditional rock and tire structures
Description: Middle East's largest artificial reef farm, launched 2019; shallow reefs (11–13m) already demonstrate higher fish catch rates than adjacent deep natural reefs; integrates modern concrete designs with traditional Omani tidal-area reef methods (rocks, pottery, palm).

Name: The Sanctuary Artificial Reefs
Location: Marine Protected Areas, Mozambique Channel, Mozambique
Size: Multiple deep-water structures; 78+ fish species documented at single reef
Design: Engineered deep-water reef structures
Description: Rapidly productive deep-water artificial reefs in Mozambique's MPAs recording 78+ fish species at a single reef; large female brindle bass up to 200 kg observed, signaling critical breeding protection function in one of the Indian Ocean's most biodiverse corridors.

Name: Morocco Anti-Trawling Reef Program
Organization: Morocco Ministry of Marine Fisheries / INRH
Location: Martil and northern Mediterranean coast, Morocco
Size: 37.5 million dirham budget; two primary reef deployments
Design: Engineered concrete anti-trawling structures
Description: Dual-purpose artificial reefs deployed to combat illegal bottom trawling and restore degraded marine habitat off northern Morocco's Mediterranean coast; combines biodiversity recovery with recreational dive tourism development.

Name: Colombia One Million Corals Program
Organization: Colombian Ministry of Environment & Sustainable Development
Location: Pacific and Caribbean coasts of Colombia (representative: Seaflower Biosphere Reserve)
Size: 735,822+ coral fragments of 30+ species (as of June 2023)
Design: Rope nurseries, metal tree structures, and MARS reef stars
Description: Largest coral restoration project in the Americas; targets Pocillopora, Pavona, Porites, and Gardineroseris species across both Colombia's Pacific and Caribbean coasts; adapts protocols to whale migration corridors and bleaching events.

Name: Sonoran Artificial Reef System (SAAS)
Organization: Mexican Navy / State of Sonora / San Carlos–Guaymas municipal government
Location: San Carlos, Guaymas, Sonora, Mexico (Sea of Cortez)
Size: 14+ naval artifacts: 7 ships, 2 helicopters, 1 aircraft, 1 amphibious vehicle, 3 artillery pieces; 12–16m depth
Design: Decommissioned Mexican Navy vessels and military equipment
Category: Military Equipment Reef / Ship Reef Complex
Description: One of Latin America's largest military-artifact reef systems; multi-phase deployments since 2020 including the 13-meter El Tanin (March 2025) and PC-208 patrol craft De La Fuente (April 2025); positioned as a replicable model for other Mexican states.

Name: The Tank
Location: Aqaba Marine Park, Aqaba, Jordan (Red Sea)
Size: M42 Duster anti-aircraft tank; 5.8m long, 3.2m wide, 2.8m tall; 6–8m depth
Design: Scuttled M42 Duster self-propelled anti-aircraft tank
Description: Single military vehicle intentionally sunk in 1999 as a shallow artificial reef and snorkel-friendly dive site; now encrusted with soft corals and sponges and used as a beginner site in Aqaba Marine Park.

Name: The Pyramids
Design: Pyramid-shaped hollow concrete blocks placed on sandy seabed
Description: Series of purpose-built concrete pyramids installed off Amed/Jemeluk to create reef habitat and offset nearby coral loss; now a marine-life-rich dive site visited for turtles, rays, morays, and macro life.

Name: Dakota Plane Wreck
Location: Paçoz Cove, Karaada, Bodrum, Muğla, Turkey
Size: C-47 Dakota military transport plane; 20m long, 30m wingspan; 19–33m depth
Design: Scuttled Turkish Air Force C-47 Dakota aircraft
Description: Military transport plane intentionally sunk off Karaada as an artificial reef and dive attraction; broken into two pieces after storms but remains a well-known Bodrum wreck site hosting groupers and schools of bream.

Name: A World Adrift
Organization: Jason deCaires Taylor / Grenada Tourism Authority
Design: Marine-grade stainless steel and pH-neutral green cement boat sculptures with coral- and sponge-nursery rigging
Description: Climate-themed underwater museum in Carriacou created after Hurricane Beryl; the boat sculptures function as artificial reefs, providing sanctuary for marine life and new nursery space for coral and sponge recovery.

Name: Museum of Underwater Art (MOUA)
Organization: Museum of Underwater Art Ltd / Jason deCaires Taylor
Location: John Brewer Reef, Great Barrier Reef, Queensland, Australia
Size: Multiple installations including Coral Greenhouse and Ocean Sentinels; roughly 4–18m depth
Design: pH-neutral geo-polymer concrete, marine-grade stainless steel, basalt, zinc, and aggregates
Description: The Southern Hemisphere's first underwater museum; the John Brewer Reef installations are habitat-forming public artworks that accumulate marine life, create refuge space, and support reef education, science, and conservation.

Name: Museum of Underwater Sculpture Ayia Napa (MUSAN)
Organization: Municipality of Ayia Napa / Department of Fisheries and Marine Research / Jason deCaires Taylor
Design: Inert pH-neutral cement and stainless steel sculptures forming an underwater forest
Description: World's first underwater forest and an artificial reef within a Marine Protected Area, designed to transform a barren sandy stretch into a thriving marine habitat and reef-like biodiversity hotspot.

Name: Cannes Underwater Eco-Museum
Organization: Jason deCaires Taylor / City of Cannes
Design: pH-neutral cement and marine-grade stainless steel sculptural portraits with habitat-forming bases
Description: Mediterranean underwater museum positioned in the protected southern waters of Île Sainte-Marguerite; the sculptures' textured surfaces and habitat-forming foundations attract marine fauna and flora and support site regeneration.

Name: Nexus
Organization: Christian Ringnes / Sjøholmen Children’s Art Centre / Jason deCaires Taylor
Size: 12 sculptures above and below the waterline; about 2m depth
Design: pH-neutral micro cement, polyurethane foam, bronze, and stainless steel
Description: Cold-water underwater sculpture trail in the Oslo Fjord that encourages marine life and regeneration in a threatened urban fjord environment; the works are already being colonized by mussels, sea squirts, lobsters, and crabs.

Name: Coralarium
Organization: Fairmont Maldives Sirru Fen Fushi / Jason deCaires Taylor
Location: Sirru Fen Fushi, Shaviyani Atoll, Maldives
Size: Semi-submerged tidal gallery with about 30 hybrid sculptures; 3–5m depth
Design: 316 marine stainless steel, pH-neutral cement, and jesmonite
Description: Semi-submerged art reef and coral-regeneration installation in the Maldives, designed as a foundation for a new ecosystem and artificial-reef habitat, though the original sculptures were removed in September 2018 and replacement has been discussed.

Name: Nest
Organization: BASK Gili Meno / Jason deCaires Taylor
Size: 48 life-size figures in shallow water; about 4m depth
Design: Stainless steel, pH-neutral cement, basalt, and aggregates
Description: Shallow-water sculpture ring off Gili Meno created as a man-made reef, designed for marine colonization by soft corals, sponges, and later hard corals, with the goal of forming a fully established reef.

Name: Ocean Atlas
Organization: Bahamas Reef Environment Educational Foundation (BREEF) / Jason deCaires Taylor
Location: Clifton Heritage National Park, Nassau, Bahamas
Design: Stainless steel, pH-neutral cement, basalt, and aggregates
Description: Record-setting underwater sculpture off western New Providence, built to create an artificial reef for marine life to colonize while diverting tourism away from overstressed natural reef areas.

Name: Uljin Marine Ranch
Organization: Korea Fisheries Resources Agency (FIRA) / Ministry of Oceans and Fisheries
Design: Artificial reefs and seaweed beds integrated with fisheries enhancement infrastructure
Description: Marine ranch project in Uljin combining artificial reefs, seaweed beds, and fisheries enhancement to support both resource recovery and marine tourism.

Name: Bukjeju Marine Ranch
Organization: Korea Fisheries Resources Agency (FIRA) / Ministry of Oceans and Fisheries
Location: Northern Jeju, Jeju Province, South Korea
Design: Artificial reefs and habitat enhancement structures
Description: Marine ranch project in northern Jeju built to preserve marine ecosystems, enhance fishery resources, and support recreation and tourism.

Name: Wuzhizhou Island Marine Ranch
Organization: Wuzhizhou Island Tourism Zone / Hainan marine authorities
Size: Tropical national marine ranch demonstration area
Design: Artificial reefs, ship reefs, and coral transplantation
Description: Major tropical marine ranch off Sanya using artificial reefs, ship reefs, and coral restoration to rebuild habitat and support marine conservation.

Name: Dapeng Bay Marine Ranch
Organization: Shenzhen municipal authorities / marine ranch program
Design: Artificial reefs, species release, and coral habitat restoration
Description: Coral-focused marine ranch in Shenzhen combining artificial reef deployment with coral protection and stock enhancement.

Name: Nanri Island Artificial Reef Project
Organization: Putian marine authorities / local fishery agencies
Location: Off Nanri Island, Putian, Fujian, China
Design: Concrete artificial reef units for habitat and stock recovery
Description: Large artificial reef deployment near Nanri Island built to restore fish stocks, improve habitat quality, and reduce pressure on degraded fishing grounds.

Name: Genghai No. 1 Marine Ranch
Organization: Yantai marine ranch operators / Shandong marine authorities
Design: Platform-based marine ranch system with underlying artificial reef habitat
Description: Large ecological marine ranch complex in Yantai integrating artificial reef habitat with modern aquaculture and marine resource enhancement.

Name: Chiba University - Tosa Bay
Design: 3.3-ton trapezoid concrete blocks with plastic mats for seaweed transplantation
Description: Kelp-forest restoration project in Tosa Bay using 60 artificial reef units to re-establish Sargassum and associated seaweed communities after major local forest loss.

Name: JFE R&D Corporation - Jogashima
Design: Marine Blocks and ordinary concrete blocks placed near an existing Ecklonia cava bed
Description: Artificial reef trial near Jogashima testing Marine Blocks versus ordinary concrete blocks for establishing Ecklonia cava seaweed beds and evaluating substrate suitability.

Name: Fisheries Research Agency Japan - Wakasa-wan
Design: Artificial reef on sandy seabed paired with drift-Sargassum collection and seedling production methods
Description: Seaweed-forest restoration project in Wakasa-wan using a 7,000 m² artificial reef deployment and drifting-thalli recruitment methods to regenerate Sargassum habitat on barren seabed.

Name: Tokyo University of Marine Science and Technology - Sakibaru Beach
Organization: Tokyo University of Marine Science and Technology
Location: Sakibaru Beach, Amami Oshima, Kagoshima, Japan
Design: Fertilised DSCM artificial reef blocks used to enhance Sargassum growth in subtropical shallow water
Description: Subtropical seaweed-bed restoration project at Sakibaru Beach using fertilised DSCM block substrate to improve Sargassum establishment and algal growth in shallow coastal habitat.

Name: Kagoshima University - Wakasa Bay
Design: Artificial reef constructed adjacent to natural Sargassum reef to accelerate colonization
Description: Artificial reef restoration site in Wakasa Bay studying macroalgal succession and colonization by Sargassum species on engineered substrate placed next to natural reef habitat.

Name: ReefLine — Concrete Coral
Size: 22 life-sized 3D-printed concrete cars; Phase 1 of 7-mile plan
Design: 3D-printed pH-neutral concrete automobile sculptures supporting 2,200 soft corals
Description: Phase 1 of a planned 7-mile underwater sculpture park off Miami Beach, deployed Oct–Nov 2025. 22 concrete cars by Leandro Erlich, master-planned by OMA. Funded by $5M voter-approved municipal bond.

Name: Ocean Siren (MOUA)
Organization: Museum of Underwater Art / Jason deCaires Taylor
Size: 1 tidal sculpture linked to live ocean temperature
Design: Tidal stainless steel and acrylic figure with LEDs linked to water temperature sensors
Description: World's first tidal artwork linked to real-time ocean temperature data; cast from a local Wulgurukaba Traditional Owner girl.

Name: Coral Carnival
Organization: Jason deCaires Taylor / Grenada Ministry of Tourism
Design: pH-neutral cement — first use of color in underwater art
Description: 2024 expansion of Molinere Bay introducing color to underwater sculpture for the first time.

Name: Ocean Gaia
Design: pH-neutral cement — Japan's first underwater art installation
Description: Japan's first underwater sculpture, deployed 2025 off Tokunoshima. 45-ton, 5.5m-diameter work.

Name: BVI Art Reef / Kodiak Queen
Design: Porous steel kraken on scuttled WWII Pearl Harbor survivor ship
Description: 80-foot steel kraken by Mike Cline and Aydika James atop the Kodiak Queen WWII ship. Sunk 2018, funded by Richard Branson.

Name: Under SXM
Design: pH-neutral community life-cast sculptures with bespoke habitat spaces
Description: Caribbean's New Attraction of the Year 2025. 300+ life-cast sculptures from community members.

Name: MUSZIF — Museo Submarino Isla Fuerte
Organization: Tatiana Orrego / Hugo Osorio & Pedro Fuentes
Design: Zenú-inspired clay sculptures pre-seeded with baby corals
Description: Founded 2018; 25 clay sculptures inspired by pre-Columbian Zenú culture off Isla Fuerte.

Name: Casa dei Pesci
Design: Carrara marble anti-trawling sculptures
Description: Founded 2013 by fisherman Paolo Fanciulli. 24 marble sculptures that damage trawling nets. Illegal trawling has stopped completely.

Name: ART4SEA
Size: 12 installations across three islands by 24 artists
Design: Eco-friendly recycled materials: steel, glass, non-polluting debris
Description: EU co-funded multi-island underwater art (~2021). 12 installations, 12 VR experiences. UN Decade of Ocean Science.

Name: Via Crucis Underwater Museum
Size: 52 life-size statues + 8-meter Christ figure at 10m depth
Design: Concrete figurative sculptures — underwater spiritual pilgrimage
Description: 14 Stations of the Cross with 52 statues and 8-meter Christ figure at 10m depth, by Alen Kunac.

Name: Memorial Reefs International
Location: La Paz (Mexico), Venice (FL), Ocean City (MD), Mérida (Mexico)
Design: pH-balanced concrete memorial structures (no rebar)
Description: Memorial reef company; La Paz opened June 2024 in the Sea of Cortez. Revenue-driven with consumer financing.

Name: Busselton Jetty Underwater Sculpture Park
Organization: Busselton Jetty Inc. / Multiple WA artists
Size: 13 artworks at 8m depth beneath Australia's longest jetty
Design: Sculptural artworks including life-sized Southern Right Whale
Description: Opened 2023 beneath Australia's longest jetty (1865), 300+ species. Octopus moved in within 3 hours. A$1M+ Lotterywest.

Name: Underwater Museum of Art (UMA)
Organization: Cultural Arts Alliance of Walton County
Location: Grayton Beach, Walton County, Florida, USA
Size: Multiple sculptures at ~60 ft depth, 1 mile offshore
Design: pH-neutral concrete and steel sculptures by rotating artists
Description: America's first permanent underwater sculpture garden (2018). TIME 100 Greatest Places; Nat Geo Best Trips.

Name: 1000 Mermaids Project
Design: pH-neutral concrete mermaid sculptures
Description: Community-driven: individuals commission mermaid statues to commemorate loved ones at 45 ft off Palm Beach.

Name: Mazu Statue
Size: 1,800 kg sculpture + 36 pyramids with 5,000 coral transplants
Design: pH-neutral Mazu (water goddess) with coral nursery pyramids
Description: 1,800 kg Mazu sculpture (2018) surrounded by 36 pyramids holding 5,000 coral transplants.

Name: Atabey — Sosúa Bay
Design: Steel and pH-neutral cement Atabey (Taíno Universal Mother)
Description: 16-foot Atabey sculpture (2023); 700+ corals planted toward 2,500 goal. With Dominican Ministry of Environment.

Name: Living Sea Walls
Organization: Reef Design Lab / Sydney Institute of Marine Science
Design: 3D-printed biomimetic panels mimicking rock pool textures
Description: 1,000+ 3D-printed panels globally for urban marine infrastructure. Earthshot Prize 2021 finalist.

Name: Coral City Camera
Location: PortMiami, Biscayne Bay, Miami, Florida, USA
Size: 360° underwater livestream on urban coral reef
Design: Permanent underwater livestream monitoring urban coral colonization
Description: 360° livestream monitoring urban coral on PortMiami infrastructure (est. 2007). Knight Foundation and NEA funded.

Name: Alluvia
Size: Reclining figure, recycled glass with LEDs and sensors
Design: Recycled glass sculpture with integrated LEDs and environmental sensors
Description: 2024 riverine sculpture in Canterbury's River Stour, recycled glass with LEDs and environmental sensors.
